relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Joshua summons the tribes who live beyond Jordan.
He recognizes that they kept Moses' command and listened through the campaign.
They did not abandon their kin during the long unfinished work.
Now that their brothers have rest, they may return home.
Their future is still love, walking, keeping, clinging, and serving with heart and soul.
Joshua blesses them and sends them away.
The divided half-tribe of Manasseh is blessed in both directions.
The returning warriors carry wealth to divide with those who remained.
They leave Shiloh and return to Gilead.
At the Jordan they build a great altar visible from afar.
News travels before explanation.
The whole congregation gathers for war.
Instead of attacking first, Israel sends Phinehas.
Ten tribal leaders go with him as witnesses.
They cross into Gilead and speak.
The altar is interpreted as rebellion before its builders are heard.
Peor's wound is summoned as precedent.
Fear imagines today's act becoming tomorrow's wrath on everyone.
They even offer shared land if Gilead is somehow unclean.
Achan's memory intensifies the dread of one act harming all.
The eastern tribes answer the leaders directly.
They call YHWH as knower before Israel becomes judge.
They deny building a rival altar for sacrifice.
Their true fear is future exclusion by their own relatives.
Jordan might become a boundary used to deny their children a portion in YHWH.
So they built an altar that does not consume offerings.
Its purpose is witness: our children belong to the same covenant service.
The altar's shape can answer future accusations.
They reject rebellion and affirm the one altar before the tabernacle.
The explanation is good in the eyes of priest and leaders.
Phinehas recognizes that catastrophe has been averted.
The delegation returns with the clarified word.
War talk ends, and the people bless Divine instead of destroying their kin.
The altar is named Witness: YHWH is Divine across the river too.

contemplative_note: Joshua 22 is the book's great de-escalation miracle. A visible religious difference almost becomes civil war, but investigation, listening, and a credible explanation turn the altar from threat into witness. This chapter knows that belonging must be protected before suspicion becomes violence.
